subtopic_detail: Roseola - recognition of post-defervescence rash pattern
stem: A 14-month-old fully immunised boy is reviewed in a suburban general practice after his mother noticed a pink rash that appeared this morning. He had three days of high fever to 39.7 deg C that resolved overnight, during which he was irritable but feeding and alert between spikes. Today he is afebrile, playful, and back to baseline. There has been no cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, oral lesions, or diarrhoea. The rash is blanching and maculopapular over the neck and trunk with sparse spread to proximal limbs, sparing the face, palms, and soles. No lymphadenopathy is found. Capillary refill is under two seconds, mucous membranes are moist, and there is no nuchal rigidity. He attends family daycare. No household members have been unwell. He has no drug allergies. The MMR vaccine is due at 18 months and has not yet been given.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C (previously 39.7), HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Blanching pink maculopapular rash on trunk/neck, sparing face/palms/soles; no Koplik spots, no conjunctivitis, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Immunisations: Up to date for age (MMR not yet due)

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Measles with the classic prodrome having now settled into the rash phase
options.rationale: Measles requires high fever PLUS cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, with Koplik spots and a rash starting on the face/hairline spreading caudally - none of which are present here (NCIRS: Measles).

options.text: Rubella with mild post-auricular lymphadenopathy that has been missed on examination
options.rationale: Rubella typically has tender suboccipital/post-auricular lymphadenopathy with a pink maculopapular rash starting on the face and spreading caudally, often with mild fever during the rash - this child has no lymphadenopathy and the rash spares the face (RCH CPG: Rubella).

options.text: Roseola infantum due to human herpesvirus 6 with rash appearing after defervescence
options.rationale: The hallmark pattern of roseola (exanthem subitum) is 3 to 5 days of high fever in a well-appearing infant 6 to 24 months old, followed by a blanching maculopapular trunk-predominant rash appearing AS the fever resolves (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems; StatPearls: Roseola).

options.text: Erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) with the second-stage lacy rash phase
options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 starts with the characteristic facial erythema (slapped cheek), followed days later by a lacy reticular rash on the limbs - both features are absent here and the trunk-only distribution does not fit (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).

options.text: Scarlet fever with sandpaper rash and circumoral pallor evolving over the trunk
options.rationale: Scarlet fever has a sandpaper-textured rash, strawberry tongue, circumoral pallor and concurrent (not post) fever after a streptococcal pharyngitis - the texture and concurrent sore throat are absent (eTG: Group A streptococcal infection).

explanation.summary: Roseola (HHV-6) classically causes 3 to 5 days of high fever in a well-appearing 6 to 24-month-old, with a blanching trunk-predominant rash erupting AS the fever resolves (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
key_points: Fever-then-rash pattern is the defining clinical clue (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
Self-limiting; no specific treatment beyond antipyresis and reassurance (StatPearls: Roseola).
Febrile convulsions occur in around 10 to 15% of cases during the fever phase (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
explanation.pearls: If a well child has a rash AS the fever breaks, think roseola; if the rash appears DURING fever with cough/coryza/conjunctivitis, think measles.

subtopic_detail: Measles - prodrome and Koplik spots before rash onset
stem: A 3-year-old unimmunised boy presents to a regional Victorian emergency department with four days of progressive illness. He has had high fever to 39.8 deg C, a barking moist cough, profuse coryza and red watery eyes that are sensitive to light. He returned 10 days ago from a 3-week family trip to Vietnam, where he visited relatives in a rural province. He has had no rash to date. His parents declined the routine immunisations on personal grounds; he has had no MMR. He has no drug allergies and no significant past medical history. On examination he appears miserable, with a temperature of 39.6 deg C, heart rate 138, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 97% room air. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, clear nasal discharge, and on close inspection of the buccal mucosa opposite the second molars there are several 1 to 2 mm bluish-white papules on an erythematous base. There is no rash on the skin, no lymphadenopathy, and chest examination is clear.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 138, RR 32, SpO2 97% RA, BP 95/55
data_table.Examination: Bilateral conjunctivitis, coryza, productive cough, Koplik spots on buccal mucosa; no skin rash yet
data_table.History: Unimmunised; recent travel from Vietnam 10 days ago

lead_in: Which feature in this presentation is most specific for the underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Bilateral non-purulent bulbar conjunctival injection with photophobia and clear discharge
options.rationale: Conjunctivitis is one of the 'three Cs' of measles but is non-specific and also occurs in adenovirus, Kawasaki disease, and other viral illnesses (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet).

options.text: Bluish-white papules on the buccal mucosa opposite the second molars
options.rationale: Koplik spots are pathognomonic for measles, appearing 1 to 2 days before the cutaneous rash on the buccal mucosa opposite the molars; they are virtually never seen in any other condition (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet; CDNA: Measles SoNG).

options.text: Four days of persistent fever above 39 deg C in an unimmunised preschool child
options.rationale: Prolonged high fever in an unimmunised child raises concern for many infections including occult bacterial sepsis and Kawasaki disease, and is not specific for measles (RCH CPG: Fever in children).

options.text: Recent return from a 3-week family trip to a measles-endemic country
options.rationale: Travel is an important epidemiological risk factor that should prompt consideration of measles, but it is not itself diagnostically specific in an individual patient (CDNA: Measles SoNG).

options.text: Profuse coryza with a productive barking cough in a febrile child
options.rationale: Coryza and cough are part of the measles prodrome but are common to many respiratory viral infections (RSV, influenza, adenovirus, parainfluenza) and are not specific (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet).

explanation.summary: Koplik spots, found on the buccal mucosa 1 to 2 days before rash onset, are pathognomonic for measles and allow earlier diagnosis and public health action before the typical caudal-spreading exanthem appears (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet).
key_points: Measles prodrome: fever + cough + coryza + conjunctivitis (the three Cs) (NCIRS: Measles).
Koplik spots appear 1 to 2 days BEFORE the rash (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
Rash starts at the hairline/face and spreads caudally over 3 days (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
Notifiable disease - urgent public health notification required at clinical suspicion (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
explanation.pearls: Suspect-then-isolate-then-test: place the child in a negative pressure single room with airborne and contact precautions and phone the public health unit before laboratory confirmation.

subtopic_detail: Measles - public health management and contact prophylaxis
stem: A 4-year-old unimmunised child has measles confirmed on PCR after a maculopapular rash spread from the face caudally over 24 hours. He had three days of fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis before the rash, and Koplik spots were identified at the GP visit the day before rash onset. He attended a community playgroup on the day before his rash appeared and the morning of rash onset, while infectious. The public health unit has been notified and is contacting the playgroup register. Among the playgroup contacts identified within the 4 hours before to 4 days after his rash, several need urgent post-exposure management decisions. They include: a 5-month-old infant whose mother is rubella-immune but has no documented measles history or serology; a 13-month-old child who received a single dose of MMR five days ago; a 35-year-old pregnant playgroup helper at 22 weeks gestation with no documented measles immunity and a negative measles IgG on urgent serology done this morning; a 7-year-old child with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukaemia in week 2 of induction chemotherapy and currently neutropenic; and a 30-year-old playgroup leader born in 1992 with two documented MMR doses on her workplace immunisation record.

data_table.Exposure window: 4 hours before prodrome to 4 days after rash onset (still infectious)
data_table.Contacts requiring decision: 5-month infant; 13-month with one MMR five days ago; pregnant 22-week non-immune helper; 7-year-old on induction chemo for ALL; 30-year-old with 2x MMR documented
data_table.Time since exposure: Within 72 hours for all listed contacts

lead_in: Which contact most appropriately receives normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) rather than MMR vaccine as post-exposure prophylaxis?

options.text: The 5-month-old infant, given MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is not given under 6 months of age - it is poorly immunogenic due to maternal antibody; infants under 6 months exposed to measles should receive NHIG, but the option as written (MMR) is wrong for this age (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: The 13-month-old who received MMR five days ago, given a second MMR now
options.rationale: MMR five days before exposure is too recent to have generated protective immunity but a repeat dose within 72 hours of exposure may still help; NHIG is not first-line here and a single early dose is recorded but the routine schedule (second dose at 18 months) should be followed (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

options.text: The pregnant 22-week non-immune playgroup helper, given NHIG within 6 days of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is a live vaccine and CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy. A non-immune pregnant contact within 6 days of exposure should receive NHIG to attenuate or prevent maternal measles and complications; serology has confirmed she is non-immune (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles; CDNA: Measles SoNG).

options.text: The 7-year-old with ALL on induction chemotherapy, given MMR within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy; this child should receive NHIG, so the option as written is incorrect (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

options.text: The 30-year-old playgroup leader with two documented MMR doses, given a third MMR booster
options.rationale: Two documented MMR doses confer presumptive immunity; no further intervention or booster is required for this contact (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

explanation.summary: For susceptible measles contacts, MMR within 72 hours is preferred in those eligible for live vaccine, but NHIG within 6 days is indicated in those for whom live vaccine is contraindicated: infants under 6 months, pregnant non-immune contacts, and severely immunocompromised patients (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).
key_points: MMR within 72h of exposure for eligible non-immune contacts (AIH: Measles).
NHIG within 6 days for pregnant non-immune, infants <6 months, severely immunocompromised (AIH: Measles).
Two documented MMR doses count as presumptive immunity in Australia (AIH: Measles).
Measles is notifiable - phone the public health unit on clinical suspicion (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
explanation.pearls: Pregnant non-immune contacts get NHIG, never MMR - live vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy.

stem: A 6-year-old boy is in week 3 of induction chemotherapy for high-risk B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia at a tertiary paediatric oncology centre. He shared a 1-hour indoor classroom session in the day hospital school room with another child who has since been confirmed by PCR to have early varicella, with a vesicular rash that had been present for less than 24 hours during the session. The exposure was 36 hours ago. The boy has had no past episode of clinical varicella, no varicella vaccination on his record (his older sibling refused MMRV at 18 months for the family on philosophical grounds and the patient was diagnosed before catch-up), and his pre-treatment baseline varicella IgG was negative on serology drawn before induction. He is currently profoundly neutropenic with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.4 x 10^9/L, has no rash, no fever, no oral lesions, and feels well.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 96, RR 20, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Well; no rash, no vesicles, no oral lesions
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 0.4 x 10^9/L, pre-chemo varicella IgG negative
data_table.History: ALL induction week 3; significant face-to-face exposure 36 hours ago to a child with early varicella

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer the varicella vaccine within 72 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine and is CONTRAINDICATED in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Administer zoster immunoglobulin (ZIG/VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Severely immunocompromised susceptible contacts (varicella IgG negative, no prior infection or vaccination) with a significant exposure should receive ZIG within 96 hours (preferably as soon as possible, but the AIH allows extension up to 10 days in some circumstances) to attenuate or prevent disseminated varicella (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Commence prophylactic oral valaciclovir from day 7 to day 21 post-exposure
options.rationale: Antiviral prophylaxis with aciclovir or valaciclovir from days 7 to 14 may be considered as an adjunct or alternative when ZIG is unavailable, but ZIG is the preferred first-line option in this scenario per the AIH (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Reassurance and observation, treating with intravenous aciclovir only if a rash develops
options.rationale: Waiting for the rash to appear before acting forgoes the proven benefit of ZIG given before viraemia; disseminated varicella in immunocompromised children carries significant mortality (RCH CPG: Varicella; Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Send urgent varicella IgG and IgM and decide on prophylaxis based on the result
options.rationale: Repeat serology is unnecessary - the pre-treatment varicella IgG was negative, so he is documented susceptible; waiting for serology delays ZIG and reduces its effectiveness (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

explanation.summary: Severely immunocompromised susceptible children with significant varicella exposure should receive ZIG (zoster immunoglobulin) as soon as possible, ideally within 96 hours; live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in this group (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).
key_points: Significant exposure = household OR >=1 hour indoor face-to-face OR shared room OR substantial hospital contact (AIH: Varicella).
Susceptible = no past disease, no vaccination, and seronegative if tested (AIH: Varicella).
ZIG first-line in immunocompromised, neonates, and non-immune pregnant women (AIH: Varicella).
Antiviral prophylaxis day 7 to 14 is an adjunct/alternative when ZIG unavailable (AIH: Varicella).
explanation.pearls: Neonates born to mothers who develop varicella from 7 days before to 2 days after delivery also need ZIG - very high mortality without it.

subtopic_detail: Erythema infectiosum (parvovirus B19) - aplastic crisis in sickle cell disease
stem: A 9-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents to a paediatric emergency department with two days of increasing fatigue and pallor. Her older brother had a slapped-cheek facial rash one week ago, attributed at the time to a school outbreak of fifth disease. The girl herself has had a mild coryzal illness for several days and now feels exhausted with mild exertional dyspnoea. She has no acute pain, no cough, no chest pain, and no abdominal pain. On examination she is pale, with a temperature of 37.8 deg C, heart rate 122, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98% in room air, and BP 100/60. There is no slapped cheek erythema, no rash on the trunk, and no palpable splenomegaly. Bloods show haemoglobin 47 g/L (her baseline 80 g/L), reticulocytes 0.2% (baseline >5%), WCC 7.0 x 10^9/L, platelets 220 x 10^9/L, and LDH and bilirubin only mildly above baseline.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 deg C, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 100/60
data_table.Examination: Pale, mild exertional dyspnoea, no facial rash, no splenomegaly, no chest signs
data_table.Investigations: Hb 47 g/L (baseline 80), retics 0.2% (baseline >5%), WCC 7.0 x 10^9/L, platelets 220 x 10^9/L
data_table.History: HbSS sickle cell disease; sibling had slapped-cheek rash 1 week ago

lead_in: Which is the single most likely cause of her acute drop in haemoglobin?

options.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis with pooling of red cells in the spleen
options.rationale: Splenic sequestration causes sudden anaemia with a tender, enlarging spleen and is typically a complication of young children with HbSS before autoinfarction - there is no splenomegaly here, and reticulocytes would usually rise rather than fall (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Hyperhaemolytic crisis precipitated by a viral illness with rising LDH and bilirubin
options.rationale: Hyperhaemolysis would produce markedly raised LDH and bilirubin with HIGH reticulocytes; here reticulocytes are profoundly suppressed at 0.2% which excludes a hyperhaemolytic picture (UpToDate: Sickle cell aplastic crisis; StatPearls: Parvovirus B19).

options.text: Transient red cell aplasia (aplastic crisis) due to parvovirus B19 infection
options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 selectively infects erythroid precursors, causing a 7 to 10 day shutdown of red cell production; in patients with shortened red cell survival (such as HbSS), this manifests as a profound anaemia with reticulocytopenia - exactly the pattern here, with a recent sibling case providing the epidemiologic link (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; StatPearls: Parvovirus B19).

options.text: Vaso-occlusive crisis with chronic occult haemorrhage from an inflamed bowel
options.rationale: Vaso-occlusive crisis presents with pain, often in the chest, abdomen or limbs, which is absent here, and would not produce the reticulocytopenia seen on her blood film (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from poor intake in a chronically unwell child
options.rationale: Iron deficiency develops over weeks to months, does not drop the haemoglobin by 33 g/L in days, and does not cause the dramatic reticulocyte suppression seen here (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

explanation.summary: Parvovirus B19 causes transient red cell aplasia by infecting erythroid progenitors; in patients with chronic haemolysis (sickle cell, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia), the 7 to 10 day reticulocytopenia produces a life-threatening aplastic crisis (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
key_points: Hallmark labs: profound anaemia with reticulocytopenia (RCH: Sickle cell).
By the aplastic crisis phase, the patient may no longer have the slapped-cheek rash - epidemiologic link via household contacts is the clue (StatPearls: Parvovirus B19).
Treatment: red cell transfusion, supportive care; respiratory droplet isolation as patient is viraemic and infectious (RCH: Sickle cell).
Other high-risk groups: pregnant women (foetal hydrops), immunocompromised (chronic anaemia).
explanation.pearls: In a child with chronic haemolysis presenting with low Hb AND low retics, screen for parvovirus B19 - the rash may be long gone or never noticed.

subtopic_detail: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) - diagnosis and renal monitoring
stem: A 6-year-old boy presents to general practice with a non-blanching rash on his buttocks and the extensor surfaces of both lower legs for two days. He had a sore throat and runny nose 10 days ago, which resolved without treatment. Over the last 24 hours he has developed pain and swelling in both ankles, making walking uncomfortable, and his mother reports intermittent abdominal pain. He has had no vomiting, no blood in stool, and no haematuria observed. He is afebrile, with a temperature of 36.9 deg C, heart rate 96, respiratory rate 20, SpO2 99% room air, and BP 105/65 (50th centile for his height). Skin examination shows palpable purpura distributed symmetrically over the buttocks and posterior lower legs, with no lesions above the waist. Ankles are warm and tender with limited range of motion. Abdomen is soft with mild peri-umbilical tenderness, no peritonism. Urinalysis is positive for blood (2+) and protein (1+).

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 96, RR 20, SpO2 99% RA, BP 105/65 (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura on buttocks/extensor lower legs, bilateral ankle arthritis, mild abdominal tenderness
data_table.History: URTI 10 days prior; no NSAID use

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer for urgent skin biopsy of a purpuric lesion to confirm IgA deposition by direct immunofluorescence
options.rationale: HSP is a clinical diagnosis in classical presentations; skin biopsy is reserved for atypical cases where the diagnosis is uncertain (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura).

options.text: Urgent admission for empirical IV ceftriaxone and full septic workup including a lumbar puncture
options.rationale: He is afebrile, alert, and the rash distribution and morphology are classic for HSP, not meningococcaemia (which typically begins on the trunk with rapid spread and signs of shock or meningism) (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone for 4 weeks to prevent renal involvement and nephritis
options.rationale: Prophylactic corticosteroids do NOT prevent or reduce the incidence of HSP nephritis; they may be used for severe abdominal pain or arthritis, neither of which is severe here (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura; Cochrane review).

options.text: Reassurance, simple analgesia, and outpatient monitoring with regular BP and urinalysis for at least 6 months
options.rationale: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) is the diagnosis - typical age, post-URTI, palpable purpura in dependent distribution, arthritis, abdominal pain, and renal involvement. Most cases are self-limiting; management is supportive with structured renal surveillance (BP and urinalysis/UPCR) for at least 6 to 12 months because nephritis can develop or worsen after the rash settles (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura).

options.text: Arrange same-day renal biopsy given the microscopic haematuria and trace proteinuria on urinalysis
options.rationale: Renal biopsy is indicated for nephrotic-range proteinuria, persistent significant proteinuria, hypertension or rising creatinine - not for transient microscopic haematuria with trace proteinuria at presentation (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura).

explanation.summary: HSP (IgA vasculitis) is the most common childhood vasculitis - clinical diagnosis based on palpable purpura in a dependent distribution PLUS at least one of arthritis, abdominal pain or renal involvement. Management is supportive with structured renal surveillance because nephritis is the only long-term morbidity driver (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura).
key_points: Clinical diagnosis - palpable purpura on buttocks/lower limbs + arthritis/abdominal pain/renal involvement (RCH: HSP).
Most cases self-limit within 4 weeks (RCH: HSP).
Steroids do NOT prevent nephritis but may help severe arthritis/abdominal pain (RCH: HSP).
Surveillance: BP + urinalysis at 1, 2, 4, 12, 26 and 52 weeks (RCH: HSP).
explanation.pearls: Rising BP or proteinuria months after the rash has gone - that is still HSP nephritis - never declare a child 'over' HSP before completing the surveillance schedule.

subtopic_detail: Meningococcal disease - non-blanching rash recognition vs HSP
stem: A 2-year-old fully immunised girl is brought to a regional emergency department by ambulance after her parents noticed a rapidly spreading rash. Six hours ago she became unwell with fever and irritability; she has now had two episodes of non-bilious vomiting and is increasingly drowsy and floppy. On examination she is grey, mottled, and lethargic with poor central tone. Temperature 38.9 deg C, heart rate 168, respiratory rate 44, SpO2 96% on room air, blood pressure 78/40, capillary refill 5 seconds peripherally and 4 seconds centrally. There are scattered non-blanching petechiae and several larger irregular purpuric lesions on the trunk, abdomen and proximal limbs that have spread visibly while in the resuscitation cubicle over the last 10 minutes. Her neck is supple but she cries when picked up and her anterior fontanelle is no longer palpable. No focal neurology. She has had no recent URTI, no arthritis, no abdominal distension, no rash above the waist before today. Her routine immunisations including MenB and MenACWY are up to date as confirmed on the Australian Immunisation Register printout her parents have brought.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 deg C, HR 168, RR 44, SpO2 96% RA, BP 78/40, CRT 5 sec
data_table.Examination: Grey, mottled, drowsy; spreading non-blanching purpuric rash on trunk and proximal limbs
data_table.History: 6h illness; fully immunised including MenB and MenACWY

lead_in: Which is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: Obtain IV or IO access and give IV ceftriaxone without delay
options.rationale: This is meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise - rapidly spreading non-blanching rash with shock. Empirical IV (or IO if access difficult) ceftriaxone should be given as soon as access is obtained, ideally after cultures but NEVER delayed for them; fluid resuscitation, ABC support, and ICU/retrieval activation run in parallel (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection; ANZCOR/APLS).

options.text: Arrange urgent CT head and then lumbar puncture given the drowsiness and rash
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, coagulopathy, or rapidly evolving purpura; CT-then-LP delays the only intervention that changes outcome (antibiotics) and is the wrong priority (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).

options.text: Defer antibiotics until blood cultures are processed to maximise diagnostic yield from cultures
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in suspected meningococcal sepsis worsens mortality; cultures should be drawn if access is rapid but antibiotics must NOT wait for them (eTG: Meningococcal disease).

options.text: Commence IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg before antibiotics to pre-treat Waterhouse-Friderichsen adrenal failure
options.rationale: Routine steroids are not the immediate priority and do not replace urgent antibiotics; stress-dose steroids may be considered later for refractory shock under retrieval/ICU guidance (eTG: Meningococcal disease).

options.text: Reassess in 30 minutes after antipyretics as her immunisations make meningococcal disease unlikely
options.rationale: Even fully immunised children can develop meningococcal disease from non-vaccine strains or vaccine failure; the clinical picture mandates immediate empirical therapy regardless of vaccination status (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Meningococcal).

explanation.summary: Suspected meningococcal septicaemia mandates immediate IV/IO ceftriaxone alongside fluid resuscitation - antibiotics must not be delayed by imaging or laboratory workup. Cultures are taken if access is fast; otherwise antibiotics go first (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).
key_points: Spreading non-blanching rash + shock = meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise (RCH: Meningococcal infection).
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) IV/IO without delay (eTG: Meningococcal disease).
Activate paediatric retrieval (NETS or state equivalent) early (RCH: Meningococcal infection).
Antibiotic prophylaxis for household and intimate contacts via public health unit (CDNA: Meningococcal SoNG).
explanation.pearls: If the petechiae are spreading WHILE you watch, you have minutes - antibiotics now, lines + fluids in parallel, retrieval call goes in before the third bolus.

subtopic_detail: Scarlet fever - recognition and antibiotic selection
stem: A 7-year-old previously well girl is brought to general practice in Sydney with a 2-day history of fever and sore throat, and a rash that began today on her neck and chest before spreading to the trunk. She has no cough, coryza, or conjunctivitis. She is alert and well perfused. Temperature 38.6 deg C, heart rate 110, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 99% room air. Her tongue has prominent fungiform papillae visible against a coated background. Tonsils are enlarged with exudate, and there is tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. The rash is a fine, blanching, erythematous papular eruption with a sandpaper texture, accentuated in the antecubital and inguinal flexures, with relative sparing of the perioral area. She has no significant past medical history, no drug allergies, and is fully immunised. She has not travelled and lives in a non-remote setting with no Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander identification recorded.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 deg C, HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Sandpaper rash with flexural accentuation, strawberry tongue, exudative tonsillitis, tender cervical nodes, circumoral pallor
data_table.History: No allergies; non-remote setting; fully immunised

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Symptomatic care with paracetamol and reassurance, no antibiotics required at this stage
options.rationale: Confirmed or strongly suspected GAS pharyngitis with the scarlatiniform exanthem warrants antibiotics in Australia to reduce suppurative and non-suppurative complications including acute rheumatic fever (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days as a more palatable alternative
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is an acceptable alternative when penicillin V palatability is an issue, but it is not first-line in eTG and the duration should still be 10 days for GAS eradication (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg up to 1 g twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Cefalexin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy and broader-spectrum than necessary for streptococcal pharyngitis (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Oral azithromycin 12 mg/kg once daily for 5 days as a macrolide alternative
options.rationale: Macrolides are reserved for severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy due to rising macrolide resistance in group A streptococcus (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg up to 500 mg twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Scarlet fever is a clinical diagnosis of group A streptococcal pharyngitis with the toxin-mediated exanthem; in non-remote Australia, first-line is phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg (max 500 mg) BD for 10 days to eradicate the organism and reduce the risk of acute rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal sequelae (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Group A streptococcal disease

explanation.summary: Scarlet fever is group A streptococcal pharyngitis plus a toxin-mediated sandpaper-textured rash with strawberry tongue. First-line in Australia is phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg (max 500 mg) BD for 10 days (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).
key_points: Sandpaper rash + strawberry tongue + flexural accentuation + circumoral pallor = scarlet fever (RCH: GAS).
First-line: penicillin V 10 days (eTG).
Severe penicillin allergy: macrolide (eTG).
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in high-ARF-risk settings have a different regimen and stricter follow-up (RHDAustralia).
explanation.pearls: Desquamation of fingertips and palms 1 to 2 weeks after the rash is a late clue if you missed the initial illness.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Group A streptococcal disease

subtopic_detail: Kawasaki disease - diagnostic criteria and discrimination from look-alikes
stem: A 2-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department on day 6 of fever, which has been persistently above 39 deg C and unresponsive to paracetamol and ibuprofen. He has been increasingly irritable and inconsolable, with poor oral intake and reduced wet nappies, and his parents say he is 'not himself'. He has bilateral non-purulent bulbar conjunctival injection with limbal sparing, a strawberry tongue with cracked red lips, and a polymorphous truncal rash that is partly maculopapular and partly urticarial, more prominent in the perineum. There is unilateral right anterior cervical lymphadenopathy 1.8 cm in size, firm but mobile. His hands and feet are swollen with erythematous palms and soles, and his parents report he refuses to walk today. He has no cough, coryza, or genital ulcers, and no contact with anyone with confirmed measles or strep. He is fully immunised including MMR, MenACWY, and MenB. Bloods show WCC 19.2 x 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, CRP 142 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/h, platelets 612 x 10^9/L, ALT 86 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, Hb 98 g/L, and sodium 132 mmol/L. Urinalysis shows sterile pyuria.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 142, RR 30, SpO2 98% RA, BP 95/55
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, cracked lips, polymorphous rash, unilateral cervical node 1.8 cm, swollen erythematous hands and feet
data_table.Investigations: WCC 19.2, CRP 142 mg/L, ESR 78, platelets 612, ALT 86, albumin 28, sterile pyuria

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit, arrange urgent paediatric echocardiogram, and commence IVIG 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin
options.rationale: He meets full diagnostic criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever >=5 days plus >=4 of: bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, oral changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy). Treatment with IVIG within 10 days of fever onset reduces the risk of coronary artery aneurysms; aspirin is added per RCH protocol, and a baseline echocardiogram should be arranged urgently (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Take a throat swab and commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin for presumed scarlet fever pharyngitis
options.rationale: Scarlet fever has a sandpaper rash with flexural accentuation, not a polymorphous rash with extremity changes; the duration of fever (>=5 days), conjunctivitis, and oedematous hands/feet do not fit GAS pharyngitis (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone and lumbar puncture for suspected partially treated bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: There is no neck stiffness, no altered conscious state, and the systemic features (mucocutaneous changes, extremity oedema) are inconsistent with bacterial meningitis (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).

options.text: Discharge home on simple analgesia with GP review in 48 hours given his immunisations
options.rationale: A child with five days of high fever and these mucocutaneous findings cannot be discharged - delayed treatment of Kawasaki disease increases the risk of coronary artery aneurysms (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone monotherapy for presumed systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (Still's disease)
options.rationale: Systemic JIA presents with quotidian (twice-daily) fevers, salmon-pink evanescent rash, and arthritis; the mucocutaneous picture here is Kawasaki, not sJIA, and steroid monotherapy is not first-line for either (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis with risk of coronary artery aneurysms; IVIG 2 g/kg PLUS aspirin within 10 days of fever onset is the standard of care and reduces aneurysm risk from around 25% to under 5% (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).
key_points: Criteria: fever >=5 days + 4 of 5 (conjunctivitis, oral, rash, extremity, cervical node) (RCH: Kawasaki).
Incomplete Kawasaki: fewer criteria + supporting labs (elevated CRP/ESR, thrombocytosis after week 1, sterile pyuria, hypoalbuminaemia, raised ALT, anaemia) (RCH: Kawasaki).
IVIG within 10 days of fever onset (RCH: Kawasaki).
Baseline + 6-week echocardiogram for coronary artery surveillance (RCH: Kawasaki).
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria, thrombocytosis after week 1, and hypoalbuminaemia together push you toward Kawasaki even when criteria are incomplete.

subtopic_detail: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease - recognition and discrimination from HSV
stem: A 22-month-old fully immunised girl is reviewed in general practice with three days of low-grade fever to 38.4 deg C and reduced oral intake. She has been miserable and drooling. Her mother noticed today that she has spots on her hands and feet and inside her mouth. There is a known outbreak of vesicular illness at her childcare centre. On examination she is alert and well-perfused. Temperature 37.6 deg C, heart rate 116, respiratory rate 28, SpO2 99% room air. There are scattered shallow ulcers on the tongue, buccal mucosa, and posterior pharynx, and several small grey-roofed oval vesicles on an erythematous base on the palms, soles, and the dorsa of the hands and feet. There is no clustering around the lips, no gingival inflammation, and no lymphadenopathy. The nappy area also shows a few similar vesicles. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. She has had no past episode of cold sores and no immunocompromising condition.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, HR 116, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Oval grey-roofed vesicles on palms/soles/dorsa of hands and feet, shallow oral ulcers including posterior pharynx, sparse nappy-area lesions
data_table.History: Childcare outbreak; fully immunised; no prior cold sores

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Primary herpes simplex gingivostomatitis with secondary autoinoculation to the hands
options.rationale: Primary HSV gingivostomatitis causes painful gingivitis with anterior oral vesicles and lip clustering, marked systemic illness, and tender cervical lymphadenopathy - the gum-sparing, posterior-predominant ulcers and acral vesicular distribution here are wrong for HSV (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems; eTG: Herpes simplex).

options.text: Varicella with mucosal involvement and acral spread from secondary scratching
options.rationale: Varicella produces crops of lesions in different stages (macule, papule, vesicle, crust) spread mainly over the trunk and face, with much less acral predilection and no characteristic involvement of the palms and soles (RCH CPG: Varicella).

options.text: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease due to enterovirus, most often coxsackievirus A16 subtype
options.rationale: Oval grey-roofed vesicles on palms, soles, and dorsa of hands and feet, with shallow posterior-predominant oral ulcers, low-grade fever, in a toddler from a childcare outbreak is the classic picture of HFMD due to enterovirus (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).

options.text: Herpangina due to coxsackievirus with secondary autoinoculation to the hands
options.rationale: Herpangina causes posterior oral vesicles/ulcers WITHOUT acral skin lesions; the presence of typical palmar, plantar, and dorsal lesions makes hand-foot-and-mouth, not herpangina, the diagnosis (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).

options.text: Scabies with secondary bacterial impetiginisation of the wrists and feet
options.rationale: Scabies presents as intensely itchy papules and burrows in finger webs, wrists, axillae, and groin, often with family contacts also itching - the lesion morphology and the oral ulcers here are not consistent (RCH CPG: Scabies).

explanation.summary: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is an enteroviral infection (often coxsackie A16, less commonly EV71) causing low-grade fever, posterior-predominant oral ulcers and oval vesicles on palms, soles, and dorsa of hands/feet, often with nappy-area involvement in toddlers (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
key_points: Self-limiting; supportive care with hydration and analgesia (RCH: Viral exanthems).
Childcare exclusion: until all blisters have dried (NHMRC Staying Healthy).
EV71 strain can cause severe complications including aseptic meningitis, brainstem encephalitis, and acute flaccid paralysis (StatPearls: HFMD).
Differentiate from HSV gingivostomatitis: HSV = anterior/gingival, ill child, no acral lesions; HFMD = posterior oropharynx, acral lesions (RCH: Viral exanthems).
explanation.pearls: If a toddler refuses to drink because of mouth pain, accept dehydration is the real risk and admit for IV fluids - it is the only common reason HFMD comes back to ED.

subtopic_detail: Pregnant contact of parvovirus B19 - obstetric implications
stem: A 32-year-old early childhood teacher who is 18 weeks pregnant attends her GP after multiple confirmed cases of erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) were diagnosed in the kindergarten class she teaches over the last fortnight. She was in close prolonged indoor contact with two affected children one week ago, before they were excluded with their characteristic facial erythema and lacy peripheral rash. She is asymptomatic with no rash, arthralgia, or fever. This is her second pregnancy; her first was uncomplicated and the child is now in her class. Her morphology scan at 18 weeks last week was reported as structurally normal with appropriate growth and amniotic fluid volume, and she is up to date with routine antenatal care, including the first-trimester combined screen which was low risk. She has no past clinical history of slapped cheek and no documented parvovirus B19 serology on file.

data_table.Examination: Normal, no rash, gravid uterus at fundal height consistent with dates
data_table.History: 18 weeks pregnant; significant occupational exposure to confirmed parvovirus B19 1 week ago; no documented prior serology

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Administer normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) intramuscularly within 7 days of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: NHIG is not indicated for parvovirus exposure in pregnancy; there is no proven prophylactic benefit and current Australian guidance recommends serological assessment and surveillance instead (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Arrange urgent amniocentesis for parvovirus B19 PCR to detect vertical foetal transmission
options.rationale: Amniocentesis is invasive and is reserved for confirmed maternal infection with evidence of foetal compromise; it is not a first-line investigation in an asymptomatic exposed contact (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Weekly foetal middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler velocimetry from now for 12 weeks
options.rationale: MCA Doppler surveillance for foetal anaemia is appropriate AFTER confirmed maternal infection (typically weekly for 8 to 12 weeks); routinely starting it before serology in an exposed but possibly already-immune woman is inappropriate use of resources (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Maternal parvovirus B19 IgG and IgM serology to determine immune status and acute infection
options.rationale: Serology distinguishes pre-existing immunity (IgG positive, IgM negative - no further action), susceptible non-infected (both negative - repeat in 4 weeks), and recent infection (IgM positive +/- IgG seroconversion - escalate to MFM for serial foetal ultrasound looking for hydrops) (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy; KEMH: Parvovirus B19).

options.text: Reassure and discharge - parvovirus B19 has no documented effect on a pregnancy past 12 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal parvovirus B19 infection can cause foetal anaemia and hydrops fetalis with the highest risk in the second trimester (around 9 to 20 weeks); discharge without investigation is unsafe at 18 weeks (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

explanation.summary: Pregnant women exposed to parvovirus B19 need serology to determine immune status; confirmed acute maternal infection in early-to-mid pregnancy triggers MFM-led MCA Doppler surveillance for foetal anaemia and hydrops (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).
key_points: About 50% of women of reproductive age are already immune (RANZCOG).
IgG positive / IgM negative = immune, reassure (RANZCOG).
IgG and IgM both negative = susceptible, repeat in 4 weeks (RANZCOG).
IgM positive = confirmed recent infection, refer MFM for serial MCA Doppler and ultrasound for hydrops (RANZCOG).
explanation.pearls: Highest risk for foetal loss/hydrops is exposure before 20 weeks; after 20 weeks the foetus has greater haematopoietic reserve and the foetal risk drops sharply.

subtopic_detail: Eczema vs scabies vs contact dermatitis - clinical discrimination
stem: A 4-year-old girl is brought to general practice with a 3-week history of severe nocturnal itch and a worsening papular rash. Her mother and 6-year-old brother have also been itching for 2 weeks. Father has not been affected but works away from home. The girl has known atopic dermatitis maintained with emollients and occasional 1% hydrocortisone, with previous flexural distribution. Now the rash is in finger webs, wrists, axillae, peri-umbilical area, and genitalia, with sparing of the previously affected antecubital and popliteal flexures. There are excoriations, several pinpoint vesicles, and on close inspection a few short serpiginous lines on the lateral aspect of one wrist. There are no honey-coloured crusts, no flexural lichenification typical of her usual eczema, and no clear contact-pattern shape (e.g. watch strap or nickel jewellery distribution). She has not started any new soap, detergent, or topical preparation. She is afebrile and systemically well.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Papules and excoriations in finger webs, wrists, axillae, peri-umbilical, genitalia; short serpiginous lines on wrist; sparing of antecubital/popliteal flexures
data_table.History: Mother and sibling also itchy; atopic background; no new topicals

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Apply topical permethrin 5% to affected sites only, repeat if itch persists
options.rationale: Localised application misses unseen mites and is a common cause of treatment failure; whole-body (neck-down for children >2 years) application with a repeat at 7 days and concurrent treatment of contacts is required (eTG: Scabies).

options.text: Step up to topical mometasone 0.1% daily and increase emollient for presumed eczema flare
options.rationale: The new distribution (finger webs, wrists, peri-umbilical, genitalia, sparing of usual flexures) and family contagion pattern are not eczema; topical steroid monotherapy will not eradicate scabies and may worsen it (eTG: Scabies).

options.text: Refer for patch testing for contact allergens and elimination of identified household triggers
options.rationale: Contact dermatitis follows the shape of the contact (e.g. metal stud, watch strap) and has no characteristic burrows, peri-umbilical/genital involvement, or family clustering (eTG: Contact dermatitis).

options.text: Commence oral cefalexin for presumed impetiginised eczema with daily bleach bath decolonisation
options.rationale: There are no honey-coloured crusts or pustules to suggest impetigo, and the distribution does not fit secondary infection of usual eczema sites (RCH CPG: Impetigo).

options.text: Apply whole-body topical permethrin 5%, repeat at 7 days, and treat all household contacts
options.rationale: Distribution (finger webs, wrists, axillae, peri-umbilical, genitalia), nocturnal itch, family contacts also itching, and visible burrows fit scabies. First-line in Australia is topical permethrin 5% with simultaneous treatment of all household and close contacts, plus a repeat application 7 days later; bedding and clothing from the last few days are washed hot or sealed for 72 hours (eTG: Scabies; RCH CPG: Scabies).

explanation.summary: Scabies is diagnosed clinically by intense nocturnal itch, characteristic distribution (finger webs, wrists, axillae, peri-umbilical, genitalia), burrows, and contagious household contacts. Treat with whole-body permethrin 5% repeated at 7 days and treat all close contacts simultaneously to prevent ping-pong reinfection (eTG: Scabies).
key_points: Itch persists 2 to 4 weeks after successful treatment - this is NOT treatment failure (eTG: Scabies).
Bedding/clothing from preceding few days: hot wash or bag-and-seal 72 hours (eTG: Scabies).
In infants <2 years and elderly: include scalp and face (eTG: Scabies).
Crusted scabies (Norwegian) needs oral ivermectin + topical scabicide; high-risk in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities (RHDAustralia / NACCHO guidelines).
explanation.pearls: Sparing of the antecubital and popliteal flexures in a known atopic child is the giveaway that you are looking at a NEW process, not an eczema flare.

subtopic_detail: Tinea corporis vs pityriasis rosea - discrimination and management
stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to general practice with a 10-day history of an enlarging itchy rash on the right side of his trunk. It began as a single oval pink scaly patch about 4 cm across, with a slightly raised edge and clearing centre. Over the last week several smaller similar pink scaly patches have appeared on his back, both flanks, and proximal arms. He plays competitive judo and shares mats and rashguards weekly with several teammates. One teammate has had a similar-looking rash on the arm for several weeks. He is otherwise well, afebrile, and has no preceding URTI symptoms. There is no mucosal involvement. Examination shows scaly annular lesions with active erythematous scaly borders and central clearing; the original 'herald' lesion is the largest. There is no Christmas-tree distribution along the lines of Langer, no oral ulceration. Direct mycological microscopy of skin scrapings from a border lesion is positive for septate hyphae.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 86, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Multiple annular scaly lesions with raised active scaly border and central clearing on trunk and proximal arms; largest 'herald' lesion 4 cm
data_table.Investigations: Skin scraping microscopy: septate hyphae present
data_table.History: Shared judo mats; teammate with similar rash

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Reassurance, emollient, and observation for 6 to 8 weeks for self-resolution
options.rationale: This is the management for pityriasis rosea, NOT for confirmed tinea; the herald-patch-then-Christmas-tree pattern of pityriasis is mimicked here but mycology is positive, which excludes pityriasis (eTG: Tinea; StatPearls: Pityriasis rosea).

options.text: Topical terbinafine 1% cream twice daily for 2 weeks beyond the visible border
options.rationale: Microscopy confirms septate hyphae - this is tinea corporis. Topical terbinafine 1% twice daily for 1 to 2 weeks (continuing for 1 week after clinical resolution) is first-line for limited disease, with lesion-edge application extending beyond the visible border (eTG: Tinea; RCH CPG: Tinea).

options.text: Oral griseofulvin for 6 weeks given the multiple lesions and contact sport
options.rationale: Oral antifungals (terbinafine, griseofulvin) are reserved for extensive disease, scalp/hair involvement (tinea capitis), nail involvement, or topical-treatment failure - not for limited body tinea responsive to topicals (eTG: Tinea).

options.text: Topical hydrocortisone 1% twice daily for the inflammatory component plus oral antihistamines
options.rationale: Topical corticosteroid monotherapy on tinea (tinea incognito) reduces inflammation transiently but worsens fungal growth and atypical spread; antihistamines do not treat the infection (eTG: Tinea).

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and exclusion from sport for presumed superficial bacterial infection
options.rationale: Microscopy confirms a fungal cause; antibacterial therapy is inappropriate and unnecessary (eTG: Tinea).

explanation.summary: Tinea corporis is a dermatophyte infection presenting as annular scaly plaques with active scaly raised borders and central clearing, common in contact sports through shared mats and equipment. First-line is topical terbinafine 1% twice daily for 1 to 2 weeks, plus equipment hygiene and treatment of affected teammates (eTG: Tinea).
key_points: Direct microscopy of skin scrapings positive for septate hyphae confirms dermatophyte (eTG: Tinea).
Topical terbinafine, miconazole, or clotrimazole BD x 2 weeks for limited disease (eTG: Tinea).
Oral antifungal for extensive disease, scalp, nails, or topical failure (eTG: Tinea).
Contact sport: do not return until lesions are no longer scaly and cannot be covered if competing (Sports Medicine Australia).
explanation.pearls: Pityriasis rosea looks similar but is scrape-negative and follows a Christmas-tree distribution along Langer's lines - and the herald patch precedes the eruption by 1 to 2 weeks, not 10 days.

subtopic_detail: Erythema multiforme vs Stevens-Johnson syndrome - recognition and triage
stem: An 11-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department with a rash and oral pain. He started oral lamotrigine 3 weeks ago after a recent diagnosis of focal epilepsy with secondary generalisation, and the dose was rapidly titrated by his treating neurologist alongside ongoing sodium valproate, which he had been taking for 6 months. He has had no other new medications, no recent herpes simplex outbreak, and no recent Mycoplasma-like respiratory illness. Over the last 36 hours he has developed a flu-like prodrome with fever to 38.7 deg C, malaise, photophobia, and a sore mouth and eyes. Today the rash has spread rapidly. On examination he is unwell, mildly tachycardic, with temperature 38.6 deg C, heart rate 124, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98% room air, and BP 105/65. There are confluent dusky erythematous macules with central duskiness and bullae forming on the trunk and proximal limbs covering an estimated 8% body surface area, with sloughing on light lateral pressure on intact-appearing skin. He has haemorrhagic crusting of the lips, painful ulcers on the buccal mucosa and palate, bilateral red painful eyes with conjunctival injection and mucopurulent discharge, and dysuria with genital erosions visible on inspection. There are some flat lesions on the palms but no classic three-zone targets. He is alert, distressed, and able to drink only sips.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 deg C, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 105/65
data_table.Examination: Dusky macules with central blistering on 8% BSA, positive lateral skin shear (Nikolsky), mucous membrane involvement of mouth, eyes, and genitals
data_table.Medications: Lamotrigine started 3 weeks ago, rapidly titrated

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Switch lamotrigine to oral carbamazepine and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome affects younger children (typically <5 years), spares mucous membranes, and is not associated with target lesions or drug history; carbamazepine carries the same SJS risk as lamotrigine and is contraindicated (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Continue lamotrigine at half dose and start oral prednisolone for presumed erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: Erythema multiforme is characterised by classic three-zone target lesions, mostly acral distribution, and is usually post-HSV; the dusky macules with sloughing, positive Nikolsky, recent high-risk drug, and three mucosal sites here are SJS, not EM, and continuing the trigger drug is dangerous (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Commence empirical IV aciclovir and admit to a general paediatric ward for HSV-associated erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: HSV-associated EM has classic targets and minimal-to-modest mucosal involvement; the diffuse dusky macules with bullae and Nikolsky in a child on a high-risk drug fit SJS and ward-level care is inappropriate intensity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Cease lamotrigine immediately, admit to a paediatric burns/HDU service, and consult ophthalmology and dermatology urgently
options.rationale: Bullae plus positive Nikolsky sign plus involvement of at least two mucosal sites plus recent high-risk drug (lamotrigine, particularly with rapid titration or with concurrent valproate) is Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN spectrum. Immediate cessation of the offending agent, admission to a paediatric burns or HDU equivalent for fluid and skin care, and urgent ophthalmology review (ocular sequelae are the leading long-term morbidity) are mandatory (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome; RCH CPG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol, and arrange neurology review in 2 weeks for lamotrigine dose review
options.rationale: SJS has 5 to 15% mortality with delayed care, and ocular sequelae are sight-threatening - outpatient management is unsafe (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

explanation.summary: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN is a drug-induced, life-threatening mucocutaneous reaction with epidermal necrosis. Lamotrigine (especially with rapid titration or concurrent valproate) is one of the highest-risk agents. Stop the drug immediately, admit to burns/HDU, and engage ophthalmology early to prevent permanent ocular morbidity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
key_points: SJS: <10% BSA detachment; TEN: >30%; SJS/TEN overlap 10 to 30% (eTG: SJS).
Highest-risk drugs in children: lamotrigine, allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin), NSAIDs (eTG: SJS).
Mucosal involvement >=2 sites is required for SJS - eye involvement is the leading long-term morbidity driver (eTG: SJS).
Add to ADR / Lifetime Drug Alert / Australian Vaccine and Medicine Allergy Service notification (TGA).
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine titration too fast (or alongside valproate, which doubles the lamotrigine level) is the classic exam trigger - in real life, titration must follow product information to halve the SJS risk.

subtopic_detail: Atypical exanthem with shock - differentiating toxin-mediated rashes
stem: A 6-year-old previously well fully immunised girl is brought to a tertiary paediatric emergency department by her parents. Five days ago she had varicella that was treated supportively at home, with the rash now beginning to crust over and no new lesions for 48 hours. Three days ago she developed pain in her left thigh which has been progressively worsening - she now refuses to bear weight on that leg and her parents describe the pain as seeming out of proportion to the modest external skin changes they can see. Over the last 12 hours she has become febrile to 39.8 deg C, vomited twice without diarrhoea, and developed a diffuse blanching macular erythematous rash particularly on the trunk and the affected thigh. On arrival she is grey, mottled, and confused, with temperature 39.6 deg C, heart rate 178, respiratory rate 48, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 70/35, and capillary refill 6 seconds in the limbs and 5 seconds centrally. Her left thigh has a 6 x 8 cm area of dusky erythema overlying a healing varicella lesion, with marked tenderness extending well beyond the visible erythema and a small area of skin anaesthesia centrally. There is no obvious crepitus on the limb. Bloods show WCC 24 x 10^9/L with marked left shift, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L (baseline normal), ALT 240 U/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, and platelets 92 x 10^9/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 178, RR 48, SpO2 95% RA, BP 70/35, CRT 6 sec
data_table.Examination: Diffuse blanching erythroderma, dusky tender 6 x 8 cm patch on thigh overlying healing varicella, pain extending beyond erythema, focal skin anaesthesia
data_table.Investigations: WCC 24, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6, creatinine 110, ALT 240

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: MRI of the left thigh before intervention to delineate the extent of soft tissue involvement
options.rationale: Imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis - the diagnosis is surgical and operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic; resuscitation and antibiotics begin in the resus bay (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Admit, commence IVIG 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin for atypical Kawasaki disease
options.rationale: Atypical or incomplete Kawasaki disease can mimic this picture but pain disproportionate to skin findings, lactic acidosis, AKI, and the dusky soft tissue lesion overlying healing varicella are not Kawasaki; treating as Kawasaki misses life-threatening necrotising fasciitis (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Empirical IV aciclovir monotherapy for disseminated varicella with secondary haemodynamic compromise and septic shock
options.rationale: Disseminated varicella in an immunocompetent child is rare; the focal painful thigh lesion with anaesthesia and the systemic toxin-mediated picture point to invasive bacterial superinfection, not viral dissemination (RCH CPG: Varicella).

options.text: Commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and admit to a general paediatric ward for scarlet fever observation
options.rationale: Scarlet fever causes a sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and is not associated with shock, lactic acidosis, AKI, or pain disproportionate to skin findings - oral therapy and ward-level care are dangerously inadequate (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Resuscitate, give IV ceftriaxone, vancomycin and clindamycin, and call surgery for urgent debridement
options.rationale: Post-varicella, pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, shock and multi-organ derangement (acute kidney injury, transaminitis, lactic acidosis) is invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection plus streptococcal toxic shock syndrome. Broad-spectrum cover (e.g. ceftriaxone) PLUS anti-staphylococcal (vancomycin) PLUS clindamycin (toxin suppression and ribosomal action) are essential, with urgent surgical debridement the definitive intervention - antibiotics do not penetrate necrotic tissue (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection; RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).

explanation.summary: Post-varicella invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection and streptococcal toxic shock syndrome is a paediatric emergency. Recognition rests on pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, and rapid multi-organ failure. Resuscitate, broad antibiotics including clindamycin, and urgent surgical debridement (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).
key_points: Pain out of proportion + focal anaesthesia + dusky skin + shock = nec fasc until proven otherwise (eTG).
Clindamycin is critical - inhibits exotoxin production (eTG).
Definitive treatment is surgical - imaging must not delay theatre (RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).
iGAS post-varicella is a recognised pattern in Australian and international cohorts (CDNA: iGAS SoNG).
explanation.pearls: If you are not sure whether to call the surgeons, you already are - operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic and there is no safer time than now.
